steps.title: Convert gas mileage to a consumption rate
steps.content:
If the car gets 35 miles per gallon, then driving 1 mile consumes $\frac{1}{35}$ gallon. Therefore, driving $d$ miles consumes $\frac{d}{35}$ gallons.

The key is recognizing that we need to express miles in terms of gallons used, not the other way around.

steps.title: Write the function for remaining gas
steps.content:
The remaining gas equals the starting amount minus the amount consumed:
$$f(d) = 10 - \frac{d}{35}$$

steps.title: Check all choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — $f(d)=\frac{d}{35}-10$: This subtracts the starting fuel instead of the fuel used — gives negative values immediately and grows instead of decreasing ✗
  • Choice B — $f(d)=d-10$: Subtracts miles directly from gallons — units are wrong ✗
  • Choice C — $f(d)=10-\frac{d}{35}$: Starts at 10 gallons and subtracts $\frac{d}{35}$ gallons consumed — correct ✓
  • Choice D — $f(d)=10-d$: Subtracts miles directly from gallons — units are wrong ✗
That is the correct answer.
